title: Classes — reading what AI just wrote you
blurb: AI ships classes constantly: SQLAlchemy models, FastAPI schemas, custom exceptions. You don't need to design them. You need to read one without flinching.

lessons.title: class, __init__, self — the three keywords AI uses every time

steps.body:
# A class is a template

When AI generates `User`, `Order`, `ChatSession`, `Embedding`, what it's
giving you is a **class** — a small template that bundles some data with
the functions that operate on that data.

You'll rarely write classes from scratch. But you'll read them in every
real codebase: ORM models, API schemas, agent state, custom errors.

The three pieces you need to recognize:

- `class Name:` — declares the template.
- `def __init__(self, ...)` — runs once per new instance, sets up data.
- `self.something` — the per-instance data; the `self` is the instance itself.

Run the editor. `User("maya")` calls `__init__`, which stashes the name on
the new instance. `u.greet()` reads it back.

steps.prompt:
Cursor wrote the `Order` class above. Which line below is calling a
**method** (a function attached to the instance), not just reading an
attribute?

options.label: `o.total`
options.explain: `total` is an attribute. No parens, no call.

options.label: `o.with_tax`
options.explain: Without parens, you're getting the method object itself, not calling it. The call needs `()`.

options.label: `o = Order(100)`
options.explain: That's calling the class — it constructs a new instance via `__init__`. Not a method call on `o`.

steps.body:
# `self` is just the instance

The thing that confuses everyone the first time: every method in a class
takes `self` as its first argument, but you never pass it yourself.

When you write `rex.describe()`, Python silently calls `Pet.describe(rex)`.
The dot does the work. `self` inside the method is whichever instance you
called it on.

That's why two `Pet` instances can have different names without colliding:
`self.name` reads from *this* instance, not from the class. Each `Pet(...)`
gets its own `self`.

Run the editor. Two pets, two `describe()` calls, two different outputs.
The class definition doesn't change between calls — only `self` does.

hint.body: Each `Counter()` call makes a fresh instance with its own `self.value`. They don't share state.
`a.bump()` runs twice → `a.value` is 2. `b.bump()` runs once → `b.value` is 1. Output: `2 1`.

steps.code:
class Counter:
    def __init__(self):
        self.value = 0

    def bump(self):
        self.value += 1

a = Counter()
b = Counter()
a.bump()
a.bump()
b.bump()
print(a.value, b.value)
steps.prompt:
Read the editor. Two separate `Counter` instances, each bumped a
different number of times. What does the last line print?

hint.body: Inside any method, the current instance is bound to the first parameter.
It's `self`. `self.name` reads the attribute that was set in `__init__`.

steps.prompt:
Cursor wrote a `Profile` class. The `display` method needs to read the
name that was stored in `__init__`. Fill in the keyword that refers to
the current instance on line 6.

Keyword: ___

hint.body: Every method's first parameter is `self`. Without it, the method can't see instance data.
Change line 5 from `def total():` to `def total(self):`. Now `self.items` works.

steps.prompt:
Claude wrote this and it crashes with a `TypeError` because it forgot
`self` in the method definition. Add `self` as the first parameter to
`total` so the script prints `15`.

hint.body: To persist data on the instance, assign to `self.something`, not a bare local name.
Replace line 4 with `self.title = title`. Now `self.title` exists when `label` reads it.

steps.prompt:
AI wrote `__init__` but forgot to actually store the argument on the
instance — it just made a local variable that gets thrown away. Fix
line 4 so `self.title` is set, then the script will print `pyloft v1`.

hint.body: `__init__(self):` with no other params, then `self.points = 0`. The `add` method takes `(self, n)` and runs `self.points += n`.
After defining the class: `s = Score()`, two `s.add(...)` calls, then `print(s.points)`.

steps.prompt:
Write a class called `Score` that:

- Takes no arguments to its `__init__` (besides `self`).
- Sets `self.points` to `0` in `__init__`.
- Has a method `add(self, n)` that increases `self.points` by `n`.

Then create one instance, call `.add(3)` and `.add(7)`, and print
the final `points`.

Expected output:
```
10
```
steps.starter: # define the Score class, then use it

steps.prompt:
AI handed you the skeleton of a `Cart` class. The `__init__` and `add`
methods are wired correctly. Your job: finish the `total` method so it
returns the sum of all prices currently in `self.items`.

Expected output:
```
17
```
